issues.description: States 'Where f double prime changes sign, you get a point of inflection' as if a sign change is the whole story, then later (15_recap) implies the same. A reasonable learner can over-generalize the converse: that f''=0 (or f' having a local extremum) automatically means an inflection point. The lesson never warns that f'' must actually CHANGE sign, not merely equal zero or be undefined — the classic y=x^4 at x=0 counterexample (f''=0 but no inflection) is exactly the AP trap this phrasing invites.
issues.fix: Add one clause: 'but f double prime equaling zero is not enough on its own — the concavity must actually flip. f equals x to the fourth has f double prime of zero at the origin yet stays concave up, so it is NOT an inflection point.'

issues.description: The narration asserts a specific outcome ('f goes up, hits a max at x = -1, comes down, hits a min at x = 3, then turns back up') that is only valid for ONE particular sign pattern of the f-prime graph (positive, then negative, then positive). The audio never states that sign pattern, so a learner viewing a different f-prime graph — or relying on audio alone — could wrongly memorize 'first critical point is always a max, second is always a min.' That ordering is graph-specific, not a rule.
issues.fix: Tie the conclusion explicitly to the observed signs: 'Because f prime is positive before x = -1, negative between -1 and 3, and positive after 3, f rises to a max at -1, falls to a min at 3, then rises. Read the actual signs each time — the order of max and min depends entirely on them, not on which critical point comes first.'

issues.description: The strong warning 'Its y-values are slopes of f, not heights of f' is correct and valuable, but the lesson stops at first-derivative info and never mentions that the SLOPE of the f-prime graph (i.e., where f-prime itself is rising/falling) tells you concavity of f and locates inflection points of f. A learner finishing this 'read f from f-prime' segment may believe an f-prime graph cannot reveal concavity, reinforcing a 'looking at f-prime not f' blind spot.
issues.fix: Add: 'And where this f prime graph is itself rising, f is concave up; where it is falling, f is concave down — so the peaks and valleys of the f prime graph mark f's inflection points.'

issues.description: The closed-interval candidates test is run correctly, but the verification logic shown only compares numerical area values and never explicitly confirms the interior critical point is a MAX (e.g., via second-derivative sign A'' = -12x < 0). Since both endpoints give A = 0, the largest-value comparison happens to identify the max, but a learner could internalize 'the interior critical point always wins / is always the max,' which fails when an endpoint is the optimum. This slightly undercuts the lesson's own repeated 'confirm max vs min' message.
issues.fix: Add a sentence noting A'' = -12x is negative on the interval, confirming concave down (a max), and remind learners that on other problems an endpoint can be the true optimum, so the comparison — not the location — decides.

issues.description: The closing generalization 'when the constraint is symmetric, the optimum usually is too' is an imprecise heuristic stated almost as a rule. A reasonable learner may over-apply it (e.g., assume the least-material soup can from the hook, or a min-perimeter-for-fixed-area problem, yields equal/symmetric dimensions) and skip the actual optimization. The verification step (A'' = -2 < 0 confirming a max) is also omitted right after a video that hammers 'always verify.'
issues.fix: Soften to 'symmetry can hint at the answer but never replaces the calculus,' and add 'A double prime is -2, negative, confirming L = 10 is a maximum.'

notes: Core math (rectangle optimization, implicit circle tangents, f'/f'' meanings) is all correct; the weaknesses are misconception-inviting omissions — chiefly the 'f''=0 implies inflection' trap and a few skipped max-vs-min confirmations that contradict the video's own 'always verify' theme.
